runs.llm_decision_summary: The chip peak temperature (89.3°C) exceeds the limit (85.0°C). The most effective immediate action is to increase the thermal conductivity of the heat spreader to enhance lateral heat spreading and reduce the hotspot intensity. A conservative 40% increase is proposed, staying well within the allowed 2.0x step ratio.

validation_reasons: spreader_material: conductivity must stay within [0.1, 500.0], got 700.000000

runs.llm_decision_summary: The chip temperature (89.26°C) exceeds the limit (85.0°C). The previous attempt to increase spreader conductivity failed because it exceeded the upper bound of 500.0 W/(m*K). Since the material is already at its maximum effective conductivity, the next best lever is to increase the geometric cross-section of the heat spreader to improve lateral heat distribution. I will increase the spreader width by 20% (from 0.7 to 0.84), which is within the allowed 1.5x step ratio and fits within the design domain bounds.

The chip temperature (89.26°C) exceeds the limit (85.0°C) by ~4.26°C. Previous iterations likely exhausted simple material increases or hit diminishing returns. Following the strategy note to use coordinated multi-variable changes, I will simultaneously increase the heat spreader's conductivity and its width. This combines better lateral conduction with a larger surface area to dissipate heat towards the side boundaries.
validation_reasons: component heat_spreader moves outside the current envelope
spreader_material: conductivity must stay within [0.1, 500.0], got 600.000000

runs.llm_decision_summary: The chip temperature (89.26°C) exceeds the 85.0°C limit. Previous attempts to maximize spreader width and conductivity hit upper bounds. The strategy shifts to increasing the base plate conductivity, which acts as the downstream heat extraction path. Increasing base conductivity from 12.0 to 24.0 W/(m*K) (within the 2.0x step limit) will reduce thermal resistance between the spreader/chip assembly and the cooled boundaries.

The design is feasible with a chip max temperature of 58.6°C, well below the 85°C constraint. To further minimize the objective, I will increase the heat spreader conductivity. This is the highest-priority lever for improving lateral heat spreading and reducing peak temperature.
